english: 3. termin. of ṅa (nga), 'to one's self', ṅar-_odzin (ngar 'dzin) = bdag-_odzin (bdag 'dzin), selfishness, self-interest. Mil. - 4. ṅar _odón-pa (ngar 'don pa) to set on or against, to instigate, nyams-kyi ṅar _odón-pa (nyams kyi ngar 'don pa) irritations of the mind, excitements Mil.; nyam-ṅár (nyam ngar) Lex. id. (?) - 5. v. ṅar-ṅár-po (ngar ngar po).
the roaring, of lions etc., _odón-pa ('don pa), sgróg-pa (sgrog pa) Mil.; W. *táṅ-cè* (*tang ce*) also to call to, to shout at.

hoarse, husky, wheezing, e.g. in old age Thgy.; ṅar-ṅár _okún-sgra (ngar ngar 'kun sgra) a hoarse groaning Pth.; ṅar-glúd (ngar glud) hoarseness and phlegm Med.; gré-ba ṅar-ba (gre ba ngar ba) a hoarse throat Med.
v. ṅár-ba (ngar ba).
mucus, snivel, (affords food to certain demons).
stalk of plants Med.
W. strong, ferocious, of the tiger etc.

1. strength, force; hardness, of steel; cold, frost, cold wind Mil. (cf. ṅá-ra (nga ra), ṅad (ngad)); ṅar gtóṅ-ba (ngar gtong ba), W. *táṅ-ce* (*tang ce*), *ċùg-cè* (*cug ce*), Sch. also ldúd-pa (ldud pa), to steel, to temper. - ṅár-ċan (ngar can) 1. strong, vigorous 2. tempered; ṅár-ldan (ngar ldan) id.; sems ṅár-ldan (sems ngar ldan) a strong mind Mil.; ṅar-méd (ngar med) weak, soft. - 2. (v. ṅar (ngar) 1.) a sort of flap (of breeches).
1. irritable, passionate, impetuous Sch. - 2. strong, powerful, e.g. a powerful protection, Mil.

to be fatigued, tired, wearied; fatigue, weariness, resp. sku ṅál-ba (sku ngal ba), or thugs ṅál-ba (thugs ngal ba), also mnyél-ba (mnyel ba); ṅal soṅ (ngal song) I am tired; spóbs-pa ṅal (spobs pa ngal) the strength decreases Med.; ṅál-ċad-pa (ngal cad pa), ṅál-dub-pa (ngal dub pa) intensive forms of ṅal (ngal); ṅal júg-pa (ngal 'jug pa) vb. a. to tire, fatigue, weary; ṅal gsó-ba (ngal gso ba) 'to cure weariness', to rest, frq.; ṅal-stégs (ngal stegs) a rest, a sort of crutch or fork, which coolies sometimes carry with them, to support their load, whilst taking a momentary rest in standing; also any bench or seat inviting to repose. To provide such conveniences for wayfaring men is considered a meritorious act.
1. instr. of ṅa (nga). - 2. mi-ṅas (mi ngas) Tar. 37, 16. is undoubtedly a typographical error, instead of mi-nad (mi nad). Schf. has left it without an annotation.
num. fig.: 34.
num. fig.: 64.

pf. ṅus (ngus), resp. šúm-pa (shum pa), 1. to weep, 2. W. also to roar, used of swelling rivers, not of the wind; Schr.: 'to groan like a turtle-dove'; *ṅu ma ṅu* (*ngu ma ngu*) W. do not weep! ṅús-pai mċí-ma (ngus pai mchi ma) tears that have been shed Dzl.; ga-ċád ṅús-pa (ga cad ngus pa) weeping without a cause, hysterical weeping Med.; ṅú-ru júg-pa (ngu ru 'jug pa) to cause to weep Lt.; *ṅú-ma-gód* (*ngu ma god*) W. weeping and laughing at the same time; šes ṅús-so (shes ngus so) thus he said weeping Glr.; *ṅu ḍhó-wa* (*ngu dho wa*) (lit. gro-ba (gro ba)) C. to be sorrowful, sad. - ṅú(-ba)-po (ngu ba po) Cs., ṅú-mkan (ngu mkhan) col. one weeping, a weeper. - ṅú-šur-ċan (ngu shur can) Sch., ṅú-mkan (ngu mkhan) col. a child that is continually crying. - ṅu-_obód (ngu 'bod), ṅu-_ordzí (ngu 'rdzi), W. *ṅu-zí* (*ngu zi*), sbst. a crying, howling, lamenting.
W. for ṅúr-ba (ngur ba) 1.
Ts. = ṅúr-ba (ngur ba) 2, to grunt; to snore; to pur (of cats).
a sob Cs., Schr.

1. sbst. duck, esp. the red wild duck, Anas casarca; ṅúr-ka (ngur ka) as red as fire, fiery red; ṅúr-smrig (ngur smrig) yellowish red, saffron colour, the original colour of the monks' habit, though not the common high-red of the Brug-pa (brug pa) monks in Sik. and in W. - 2. vb. to grunt, of pigs and yaks.
v. ṅú-ba (ngu ba).
num. fig.: 94.

pers. pron. first person, eleg. for ṅa (nga), I, we; ṅéd-kyi (nged kyi) my, our; ṅed gnyis(-ka) (nged gnyis ka) we two; ṅed gsum(-po) (nged gsum po) we three; ṅed spun gsum (nged spun gsum) we three brothers Glr.; ṅéd-kyi bu-dód mdzod (nged kyi bu dod mdzod) have the goodness to become our foster-son Mil.; sometimes ṅa (nga)
